Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd set aside time to study, maybe 27 hours a week or so, but I rarely stuck to a strict schedule. I'd often listen to lectures while doing other things around the house, since I learn better by hearing stuff anyway. Some weeks I'd be more focused, other weeks I'd just drift through the material without much direction.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I'm 28, so I've got other stuff going on in life that sometimes takes priority over coursework. When deadlines loomed, I'd cram more, but otherwise I'd let things slide and study whenever I felt like it. Regular times weren't really my thing.
Q3: Usually a deadline or an assignment reminder would get me to log in. Sometimes I'd check if there was something new posted, but honestly, I only logged in maybe four times a week at most. It wasn't like I was eager to be there—it was more like obligation dragging me back.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a natural habit for me. I'd use it when I absolutely had to, but it never felt like part of my daily routine. I think if the content had been more engaging or if I felt more connected to the course, maybe it would've been different.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me since I'm an auditory learner. I'd watch them—well, mostly listen to them—for over an hour sometimes, letting the information sink in. Anything I could absorb by hearing was way more effective than reading dense PDFs.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums. I posted once the whole course, and that was just to meet a requirement. Reading other people's posts felt like a waste of time when I could just listen to the lecture again. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly helped me pass the exam or complete an assignment. If I couldn't see the immediate payoff, I'd probably skip it. I know that's not the best approach, but with everything else going on, I had to prioritize what actually counted.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely skippable. Required activities had a deadline and consequences, so I'd do them eventually. Optional ones? I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. There was no external pressure pushing me toward them.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also used some external YouTube videos that explained concepts better than the course materials. A lot of my studying happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was reviewing downloaded notes or re-listening to audio files I'd saved, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd spend hours going over material in my head or discussing concepts with a friend, but the system would just show me as inactive.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then work with it offline for days. The LMS would show one access, but I might've reviewed that material five or six times on my own. It's just more convenient to have things saved locally.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. My anxiety was pretty high, and I started feeling disconnected from the whole thing. I'd log in, stare at the screen, and just feel overwhelmed. The peer support intervention helped a bit, but there were definitely weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to engage.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or stressed about other life stuff, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Competing responsibilities meant coursework often got pushed to the back burner. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections entirely.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else completely. I'd scroll through materials without actually reading them. It looked like engagement on paper, but mentally I was checked out. My anxiety made it hard to focus.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. The anxiety made starting things feel impossible sometimes. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd feel the same way. It's a cycle I've struggled with for years.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. When something was due, I'd finally log in and cram. My whole LMS usage pattern was basically deadline-driven—spikes of activity followed by long periods of nothing.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. By the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally. If the feedback was negative, it just added to my anxiety without motivating me to do better. Positive feedback was nice but didn't make me more engaged overall.
Q18: The structure felt pretty standard, but it didn't really pull me in. Weekly modules were fine, but without something forcing me to participate regularly, I'd fall behind. The peer interactions requirement got me talking to a few classmates, which was actually helpful.
Q19: Having video content made things easier since I could listen rather than read. But the discussion requirements felt forced and pointless to me. Also, the sheer amount of material was overwhelming sometimes—it would've helped to have clearer priorities about what was essential.
Q20: I think that happens when people are anxious or stressed and they're trying to engage but can't actually produce work. They might be clicking around, watching videos, reading things, but when it comes to actually submitting something, there's a mental block. I've been there.
Q21: That's kind of me, honestly. My exam score was decent enough to pass, but my LMS activity was pretty minimal. I studied a lot offline and used external resources. The system doesn't capture what happens outside its walls, so low activity doesn't always mean low effort.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on something and for how long, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or understanding the material. They miss offline study, mental engagement, and the quality of learning. It's a very surface-level picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just logging in or completing tasks—it's being present and invested. For me, that was hard to maintain consistently this semester.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how much anxiety affected my participation, or how much I actually learned from offline studying. It wouldn't capture the conversations I had with peers outside the platform, or the times I was genuinely trying but couldn't focus. There's a whole internal experience that numbers can't measure.
